caption: Fig. 1 . Fig.1. XRD profiles of raw coals.
text: Fig. 1 . Fig.1. XRD profiles of raw coals. Prior to XRD analysis, the coal samples were stirred in 5 M HF for 24 h at ambient temperature to remove the peaks of mineral matters. The samples were then stirred in 5 M HCl for 24 h at ambient temperature and washed with pure water. In the previous study, we confirmed that the influence of the demineralization treatment on the stacking-structure parameters determined by the STAC-XRD was negligible [7] . In STAC-XRD, the fraction of the stacking structure (P s ), the distribution of the number of aromatic layers in the stacking structure (distribution of N), and the average number of N (N ave ) were estimated by analysis of the intensity of the 002 peak of carbon. P s has been defined by Shiraishi [11] as the ratio of the number of carbon atoms in stacked structures to the number of carbon atoms in the entire sample. The distribution of N was calculated by a Fourier transform method [4] . The XRD profiles of the pulverized samples were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Rotaflex RU-300) with Cu K a radiation by a step-scanning method in the range of 2qZ 10-418. After the XRD profiles were corrected with adsorption, polarization, and atomic scattering factors, the parameters with respect to the stacking structure were estimated. Details of the STAC-XRD method have been reported previously [6] . The interlayer spacing of aromatic layers (d 002 ) in the samples was calculated from the peak position by means of Bragg's equation. The mean crystallite size along the c axis (L c ) was calculated from the width at the half maximum of the 002 peak using Scherrer's equation. The average number of N (N c ) was estimated from d 002 and L c by means of the following equation [10] : Fig. 1 shows the XRD profiles of the demineralized raw coals.

caption: Fig. 4 . Fig.4. N ave values of raw coals.
text: Fig. 4 . Fig.4. N ave values of raw coals. In STAC-XRD, the fraction of the stacking structure (P s ), the distribution of the number of aromatic layers in the stacking structure (distribution of N), and the average number of N (N ave ) were estimated by analysis of the intensity of the 002 peak of carbon. P s has been defined by Shiraishi [11] as the ratio of the number of carbon atoms in stacked structures to the number of carbon atoms in the entire sample. The distribution of N was calculated by a Fourier transform method [4] . The XRD profiles of the pulverized samples were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Rotaflex RU-300) with Cu K a radiation by a step-scanning method in the range of 2qZ 10-418. After the XRD profiles were corrected with adsorption, polarization, and atomic scattering factors, the parameters with respect to the stacking structure were estimated. Details of the STAC-XRD method have been reported previously [6] . The interlayer spacing of aromatic layers (d 002 ) in the samples was calculated from the peak position by means of Bragg's equation. The mean crystallite size along the c axis (L c ) was calculated from the width at the half maximum of the 002 peak using Scherrer's equation. The average number of N (N c ) was estimated from d 002 and L c by means of the following equation [10] : Fig. 4 shows the N ave values for the raw coals calculated from the distribution of N. The N ave values (Fig. 4 ) of the higher-rank coals differed from the N c values, whereas the variations in N ave and N c with carbon content were in agreement.

caption: Fig. 5 . Fig. 5. P s values of caking (PI, MI) and non-caking (EB) coals as a function of heat-treatment temperature.
text: Fig. 5 . Fig. 5. P s values of caking (PI, MI) and non-caking (EB) coals as a function of heat-treatment temperature. Fig. 5 shows the P s values of caking (PI, MI) and noncaking coals (EB) as a function of heat-treatment temperature. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 5 , the change of P s of the caking coals at the fluidity stage was small.

caption: Fig. 6 . Fig. 6. N ave values of caking (PI, MI) and non-caking (EB) coals as a function of heat-treatment temperature.
text: Fig. 6 . Fig. 6. N ave values of caking (PI, MI) and non-caking (EB) coals as a function of heat-treatment temperature. Fig. 6 shows the N ave values of caking (PI, MI) and noncaking coals (EB) as a function of heat-treatment temperature.

caption: Fig. 7 . Fig. 7. Influence of heating rate at 920 8C on P s and N ave values of BS, EB, PI, and NL coals.
text: Fig. 7 . Fig. 7. Influence of heating rate at 920 8C on P s and N ave values of BS, EB, PI, and NL coals. Fig. 7 shows the influence of heating rate at 920 8C on the P s and N ave values of coals.
